high_value_elements: Names chronic pain and reduced quality of life as the primary presenting problem
Identifies that the current situation (no analgesia) represents a pain management failure, not just a CKD management success
Recognizes concurrent CKD, hypertension, and anemia as modifiers of the pain management approach
Identifies grooming dysfunction and altered behavior as pain indicators in cats
Notes borderline blood pressure control as relevant to the management plan
major_concerns: Lists CKD or hypertension as the top priority without acknowledging pain as the presenting reason
Fails to identify inadequate analgesia as an active problem
Does not recognize feline-specific pain indicators (grooming, perch avoidance, reduced interaction)
Frames quality of life as secondary or irrelevant

high_value_elements: Prioritizes pain assessment and quality of life improvement as the immediate goal of the consultation
Addresses concurrent disease management (CKD, hypertension) as modifiers that inform but do not override the analgesic plan
Sequences monitoring plan appropriately given CKD risk
Balances short-term comfort with long-term renal safety in a structured way
major_concerns: Prioritizes renal protection over quality of life without engaging with the patient's actual presentation
Defers all pain decisions pending further diagnostics without justification
Treats all concurrent diseases as equally urgent without clinical ranking

high_value_elements: Interprets available diagnostics accurately (IRIS Stage 2, borderline BP, mild anemia)
Understands that current CKD stage (Stage 2) does not absolutely contraindicate all analgesic intervention
References the tension between NSAID-associated renal risk and the fact that uncontrolled pain also impairs renal perfusion
Considers gabapentin as a first-line adjunct safe in CKD cats with dose adjustment for anemia/GFR
Considers buprenorphine as appropriate for acute or breakthrough pain in cats
Recognizes that total T4 within normal range effectively rules out hyperthyroidism as contributor
major_concerns: Refuses to consider any NSAID without engaging with risk-benefit or specialist guidance
Proposes restarting meloxicam at previous dose without modification or monitoring
Fails to recognize gabapentin as a viable option
Does not engage with IRIS or specialist-level guidance on NSAID use in CKD Stage 2
Cannot interpret the available diagnostic data in a clinically meaningful way

high_value_elements: Proposes multimodal approach including at least two of: gabapentin, buprenorphine, low-dose NSAID with monitoring, or physical rehabilitation
Recommends environmental modifications (ramps, low-sided litter box, heated bed, non-slip surfaces)
Addresses owner education on pain recognition in cats
Sets clear quality of life goals with owner
Proposes structured recheck schedule with renal panel and reassessment of pain indicators
Considers nutritional support given mild weight loss and muscle wasting
Discusses return-to-grooming and activity milestones as outcome measures
major_concerns: Provides only one analgesic agent in a case clearly requiring multimodal management
Ignores non-pharmacologic interventions entirely
Fails to include any monitoring plan
Does not address owner compliance or home administration logistics
Recommends a drug with significant renal risk (e.g., full-dose NSAID) without a safety net

high_value_elements: Articulates specific renal monitoring parameters (creatinine, BUN, USG, UPC) and frequency
Defines thresholds at which analgesic protocol would be modified or discontinued
Acknowledges gabapentin sedation risk and the importance of starting low and titrating
Notes that untreated pain itself creates physiologic stress and may worsen renal perfusion
Discusses signs of analgesic inadequacy for owner to monitor at home
Acknowledges uncertainty regarding long-term outcomes in an older CKD cat
major_concerns: Presents no monitoring plan or safety thresholds
Does not acknowledge any risk from chosen drugs
Cannot articulate what renal deterioration would look like or when to stop
Treats the decision as straightforward when it is explicitly ambiguous
Fails to mention that untreated pain has its own physiologic costs

high_value_elements: Demonstrates awareness of specialist-level guidance (IVAPM, IRIS, WSAVA) on analgesic use in CKD cats
Explains why withholding all analgesia is not a defensible specialist position
Articulates the pharmacologic rationale for each chosen agent
Navigates the CKD-analgesia tension deliberately and explicitly rather than defaulting to avoidance
Considers quality of life as a primary clinical outcome, not a secondary consideration
Demonstrates nuance: there is no perfect answer here, and acknowledging that is appropriate
major_concerns: Defaults to 'no analgesia because CKD' without specialist-level engagement
Cannot explain drug choices pharmacologically
Does not demonstrate awareness of relevant specialist guidance
Treats the case as simpler than it is (no acknowledgment of genuine ambiguity)
Uses purely generalist reasoning without specialist framing
